   180	  - MuSR: "few models achieve better than random performance" — the OLLv2
   180	  - MuSR: "few models achieve better than random performance" — the OLLv2
   181	    docs' own words, at ALL scales it hosts [LIT-BACKED: OLLv2 about page,
   182	    2024]. Exclude below R-3.
   183	  - MATH Lvl-5 and GPQA: at ≤2B these sit at ~0 and ~chance respectively on
   184	    the public leaderboard; **UNVERIFIED as specific numbers** (not fetched
   185	    per-model this session) — but GSM8K 1.4–31.0 at our rungs bounds MATH
   186	    Lvl-5 from above, and GPQA is designed to be expert-hard. Exclude below
   187	    R-3 [EXTRAPOLATION].
   188	- CONCLUSION (confirms the REVIEW-CITED claim at source): **OLLv2 is the right
   189	  R-3+ anchor family and floors out at the programme's 100M–2B rungs** except
   190	  IFEval (usable from R-1 with an instruct host) and BBH-as-diagnostic-only.
   191	  The rev-2 provisional D1–D7 table's R-3+ placements are correct as written
   192	  [STIPULATED].
   193	
   194	## 4. Contamination: what detection can and cannot do, and how sealed benchmarks are produced
   195	
   196	**Detection limits (the review's skepticism is literature-confirmed).**
   197	
   198	- LOAD-BEARING: simple variations of test data — paraphrase, translation —
   199	  defeat n-gram/string-match decontamination: a 13B model trained on rephrased
   200	  test sets reached GPT-4-level scores on MMLU/GSM8K/HumanEval while passing
   201	  standard decontamination; their stronger LLM-based decontaminator then found
   202	  8–18% of HumanEval overlapping in RedPajama-1T/StarCoder-Data, and
   203	  contamination even inside GPT-3.5/4-generated synthetic data [LIT-BACKED:
   204	  Yang, Chiang, Zheng, Gonzalez, Stoica, arXiv:2311.04850, 2023].
   205	- LOAD-BEARING: contamination can be PROVEN (not just screened) from black-box
   206	  log-probabilities via exchangeability — a contaminated model assigns higher
   207	  likelihood to the benchmark's canonical ordering than to shuffles; works down
   208	  to 1.4B models, 1,000-example sets, few duplications — but requires logprob
   209	  access and duplication-rate knowledge, and a NEGATIVE result proves nothing
   210	  [LIT-BACKED: Oren, Meister, Chatterji, Ladhak, Hashimoto, arXiv:2310.17623,
   211	  2023 (ICLR 2024 — venue not restated on the arXiv page; UNVERIFIED as venue)].
   212	- Design consequence [STIPULATED]: the §2.2 screener (n-gram + embedding) is a
   213	  gross-leakage gate ONLY — the literature says paraphrase-level leakage passes
   214	  it, which is exactly why the rev-2 design made the sealed evaluation (§2.2a)
   215	  mandatory rather than optional. For OUR stores (which we control) the Oren
   216	  test is inapplicable (it detects training-data memorization); the screener +
   217	  authoring provenance rules carry that side. For DONOR WEIGHTS, the Oren test
   218	  is a cheap, runnable diagnostic on any open comparator with logprob access —
   219	  add it to the P3-D-SEAL toolbox.
   220	
   221	**Producing a sealed/refreshed held-out benchmark (three verified recipes).**
   222	
   223	- LOAD-BEARING (parallel-replica recipe): GSM1k built 1,000 NEW human-written
   224	  problems matched to GSM8K on human solve rates, solution step counts, and
   225	  answer magnitudes, kept the set private, and measured overfitting as the
   226	  GSM8k→GSM1k gap: drops up to 8%, systematic across some model families,
   227	  minimal at the frontier, with the gap correlating (Spearman r² = 0.36) with
   228	  the model's probability of *generating* GSM8k items verbatim [LIT-BACKED:
   229	  Zhang et al., arXiv:2405.00332, NeurIPS 2024 D&B].
   230	- LOAD-BEARING (refresh recipe): LiveBench adds/updates questions monthly from
   231	  fresh sources (competitions, arXiv, news), scores against objective ground
   232	  truth without LLM judges, and versions each release; ICLR 2025 Spotlight
   233	  [LIT-BACKED: White et al., arXiv:2406.19314, ICLR 2025]. The live site
   234	  fetch returned no content this session (livebench.ai rendered empty to the
   235	  fetcher) — current release ID **UNVERIFIED**; pin the release at P3-D-SEAL
   236	  freeze from the GitHub tags, which the review itself cited.
   237	- LOAD-BEARING (structural-resistance recipe): SWE-bench Pro's held-out set +
   238	  strong-copyleft sourcing, verified in the prior fact sheet [LIT-BACKED:
   239	  reports/lit-eval-benchmarks.md §3.5, arXiv:2509.16941, 2025].
   240	- Recipe synthesis for P3-D-SEAL [STIPULATED]: (i) for D4/D5 procedural
   241	  domains, a PINNED GENERATOR with fresh seeds per release is the cheapest
   242	  sealed producer (CLUTRR is literally built for this — generator +
   243	  configurable splits [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:1908.06177, EMNLP 2019]); (ii) for
   244	  knowledge/instruction domains, GSM1k-style matched authoring with the
   245	  difficulty-matching triple (solve rate, step count, answer magnitude) as the
   246	  consistency-band instrument; (iii) LiveBench-style versioning discipline —
   247	  every refresh is a new version, never folded in (ASM-0812 verbatim). The
   248	  GSM1k measured gap (≤8% for honest models) gives the first EMPIRICAL
   249	  calibration for the pre-registered frozen-vs-sealed consistency band: a gap
   250	  well beyond that range on matched-difficulty items is evidence of
   251	  suite-specific adaptation, not noise [EXTRAPOLATION — band constant to be
   252	  set in P3-D-SEAL with our own variance estimates].
   253	
   254	## 5. Proxy-rung validity: when a cheap rung predicts a dearer one
   255	
   256	The question P3-D-POWER and the G5 gate need answered: do R-0/R-1 measurements
   257	predict R-2+? The literature's answer is **metric-dependent**, in a way that
   258	directly shapes which metrics enter low rungs.
   259	
   260	- LOAD-BEARING: apparent emergence (sharp capability jumps that would make
   261	  small-scale results non-predictive) is largely an artifact of nonlinear/
   262	  discontinuous metrics (exact match, MC accuracy); under continuous metrics
   263	  the same models improve smoothly and predictably [LIT-BACKED: Schaeffer,
   264	  Miranda, Koyejo, arXiv:2304.15004, 2023 (NeurIPS 2023 — venue not restated
   265	  on the arXiv page)].
   266	- LOAD-BEARING: the mechanism for WHY downstream MC accuracy resists scaling
   267	  prediction: performance is computed from log-likelihoods through a chain of
   268	  transformations (normalize, compare against specific incorrect choices) that
   269	  progressively degrades the statistical relationship with scale — probability
   270	  mass on the INCORRECT options fluctuates unpredictably [LIT-BACKED:
   271	  Schaeffer, Schoelkopf, Biderman et al., arXiv:2406.04391, 2024].
   272	- LOAD-BEARING: nevertheless, aggregate capabilities ARE predictable
   273	  observationally: ~100 public models define a low-dimensional capability
   274	  space in which "emergent" phenomena, agent performance, and even the value
   275	  of post-training techniques (CoT, self-consistency) follow smooth sigmoids
   276	  predictable from smaller/weaker models [LIT-BACKED: Ruan, Maddison,
   277	  Hashimoto, arXiv:2405.10938, NeurIPS 2024 spotlight].
   278	- Corroborating instance: BBH tasks that looked flat ("emergent") under
   279	  direct prompting became smoothly improvable with CoT — the metric/method,
   280	  not the underlying capability, produced the cliff [LIT-BACKED: Suzgun et
   281	  al., arXiv:2210.09261, 2022].
   282	- Design consequences [STIPULATED]:
   283	  1. **R-0/R-1 components should be continuous or minimal-pair-logprob metrics**
   284	     (held-out LM loss; BLiMP/EWoK forced-choice logprob), which carry
   285	     predictive signal upward; MC accuracy at a rung where the model is near
   286	     floor carries almost none. This retroactively validates rev-2's R-0 row
   287	     (LM loss + BLiMP/EWoK) and the MMLU-cloze drop.
   288	  2. **Proxy-rung validity must be CLAIMED per metric family, not per suite**:
   289	     the G5 two-size confirmation should test the margin's direction on the
   290	     continuous metrics first, and treat cross-rung MC-accuracy extrapolation
   291	     as unsupported by default.
   292	  3. For P3-D-POWER: Δ_max computations at R-1 on near-floor MC benchmarks are
   293	     unstable (the baseline accuracy term a_b^cov is noise near chance) — the
   294	     power table should exclude below-floor benchmarks from κ-weighted gain
   295	     projections rather than average them in [STIPULATED].
   296	
   297	## 6. Per-benchmark floors/ceilings at tiny scale (the D1–D7 evidence table)
   298	
   299	The rev-2 provisional domain table (programme doc §1.1), now annotated with
   300	verified floors/ceilings. "Tiny scale" = the programme's 135M–1.7B hosts (and,
   301	for D1, the BabyLM ≤100M-word training regime as the closest published probe
   302	of very small capability).
   303	
   304	| Benchmark | Verified construction | Floor/ceiling at tiny scale | Verdict for the index |
   305	|---|---|---|---|
   306	| **BLiMP** | 67 paradigms × 1,000 minimal pairs, forced-choice by sentence logprob, chance 50% [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:1912.00582, TACL 2020] | GPT-2 81.5 / LSTM 69.8 / 5-gram 61.2 / human 88.6 individual, 96.4 aggregate [LIT-BACKED: aclanthology.org/2020.tacl-1.25 via paper PDF]; BabyLM-2024 10M-word baselines 69.8, best 81.2; 100M-word best 86.1 [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2412.05149, CoNLL 2024] | **KEEP at R-0/R-1 (D1)**: discriminative across the whole tiny range, ceiling pinnable at human 88.6/96.4 |
   307	| **EWoK** | 4,374 items, 11 domains, minimal pairs-of-pairs (context×target), chance 50%, logprob-scored [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2405.09605, TACL 2025 + ewok-core pages] | All tested 1.3B–70B models below human; social domains highest, physical/spatial lowest [LIT-BACKED: same]; BabyLM-2024: baselines 50.7–51.9 (≈chance), best submission 58.4 — "most submissions perform near chance" [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2412.05149] | **KEEP at R-0 with a floor warning**: at ≤100M-word training it is nearly floored; expect low headroom at 135M-param scale too; its value is the domain vector (physical/spatial deficit is exactly a world-knowledge probe), not the scalar [STIPULATED] |
   308	| **CLUTRR** | Procedural kinship-graph generator; systematic (held-out rule combinations) + robustness splits; text vs symbolic-graph input arms [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:1908.06177, EMNLP 2019; licence CC-BY-NC-4.0 per reports/lit-eval-benchmarks.md] | Paper-era: BERT/MAC on text ≪ GAT on symbolic graphs for generalization+robustness [LIT-BACKED: same]. Published floors for MODERN small LMs (zero/few-shot, 135M–2B): **not found this session — UNVERIFIED/OPEN** | **KEEP (D4)**, generator-pinned; but the R-1 floor must be MEASURED by us in P3-E-CAL, not assumed. NC licence constrains artifact redistribution [LIT-BACKED: fact sheet §1.1] |
   309	| **RuleTaker / ProofWriter** | NL rulebases; fine-tuned transformers reach 99% in-distribution and 95%+ on deeper unseen depths [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2002.05867, IJCAI 2020]; ProofWriter adds proof generation + abduction, +9% over RuleTaker, depth/OOD generalization, open+closed world variants [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2012.13048, Findings ACL 2021] | The 95–99% figures are FINE-TUNED ~355M-class encoder/T5 models — i.e., the tasks are LEARNABLE at small scale with supervision; zero/few-shot floors for small decoder LMs: **not published where we looked — UNVERIFIED/OPEN** | **KEEP (D4)** with the fine-tune-vs-prompt distinction made explicit in the index pin: as a PROMPTED index component it will likely floor at R-1; as a diagnostic with light adaptation it has near-100% ceiling — the gap itself is informative [STIPULATED] |
   310	| **FOLIO** | 1,430 expert-written conclusions / 487 premise sets, FOL-verified; labels True/False/Unknown (3-way, chance ≈33%); CC-BY-SA-4.0 [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2209.00840 + aclanthology.org/2024.emnlp-main.1229 (EMNLP 2024) + github.com/Yale-LILY/FOLIO] | "A subset remains a challenge for GPT-4" [LIT-BACKED: same] — at ≤1.7B expect ≈chance | **R-3+ or oracle-diagnostic only**; too hard as a tiny-scale index component; its NL↔FOL pairs are valuable P3-LR-PARSE material [STIPULATED] |
   311	| **IFEval** | 25 verifiable instruction types, ~500 prompts, strict/loose × prompt/inst metrics, floor ≈0 [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2311.07911, 2023] | 135M-Instruct 29.9 (card avg), 1.7B-Instruct 56.7 [LIT-BACKED: SmolLM2 cards, 2025] | **PROMOTE: usable from R-1** (not just R-3 as the provisional table had it) on instruct hosts; pin ONE metric variant [STIPULATED] |
   312	| **MMLU-Pro** | 10-choice (chance 10%), reasoning-heavier, 16–33% harder than MMLU, prompt sensitivity 4–5%→2% [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2406.01574, NeurIPS 2024 D&B Spotlight] | 1.7B-Instruct 19.3; Llama-1B 12.7 (≈3 pp over chance) [LIT-BACKED: SmolLM2-1.7B card, 2025] | **R-3+ only**, confirming the rev-2 placement and the MMLU-cloze drop |
   313	| **BBH** | 23 hard BIG-Bench tasks chosen where prior models < average human rater; CoT lifts substantially (Codex > human on 17/23); few-shot-no-CoT "substantially underestimates" capability [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2210.09261, 2022] | 135M-Instruct 28.2 / 1.7B-Instruct 32.2 (3-shot), against a mixed 2–19-choice floor ≈25–30 — near-floor across our rungs [LIT-BACKED: SmolLM2 cards + OLLv2 num_choices list] | **R-3+**; also note BBH scores are STRATEGY-confounded (CoT vs not) — the index pin must fix the prompting strategy per rung [STIPULATED] |
   314	| **BBEH** | Each BBH task replaced by a harder same-capability probe because "state-of-the-art models achieve near-perfect scores on many BBH tasks"; best general-purpose model 9.8 harmonic-mean acc, best reasoning model 44.8 [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2502.19187, 2025] | Floors out even for frontier general models | **NOT for the tiny-scale index at all**; candidate only for a far-future R-4+ version [STIPULATED] |
   315	| **GSM8K** | (fact sheet, verified 2026-07-10) 1,319-item test, EM metric [LIT-BACKED: reports/lit-eval-benchmarks.md §2.9] | 1.4 / 3.2 / 31.0 base (5-shot); 48.2 1.7B-Instruct [LIT-BACKED: same + 1.7B card] | **D5 enters at the ≥1B rung**; below that it is a floor-cell that only dilutes the index; ALSO contamination-suspect (GSM1k §4) — pair it with the sealed D5 generator leg [STIPULATED] |
   316	
   317	Cross-cutting ceiling note: chance floors are now verified per benchmark (50%
   318	BLiMP/EWoK; ~33% FOLIO; 10% MMLU-Pro; per-subtask 2–19-choice BBH/MuSR; ≈0
   319	IFEval/GSM8K/MATH), which is everything the normalization formula needs; the
   320	CEILING side has verified anchors only for BLiMP (88.6/96.4) — every other
   321	ceiling must be pinned by P3-D-INDEX from named reference models [STIPULATED].
   322	
   323	## 7. What actually beat matched baselines (skeptical accounting)
   324	
   325	The review asked every lit-bead to judge wins under their accounting. In the
   326	evaluation-methodology literature itself the load-bearing "wins" are:
   327	
   328	- **Compute-optimal test-time scaling beat best-of-N and beat bigger models at
   329	  matched FLOPs**: >4× efficiency over best-of-N; a smaller model with
   330	  compute-optimal test-time strategies outperformed a 14× larger model in
   331	  FLOPs-matched evaluation [LIT-BACKED: Snell, Lee, Xu, Kumar, ICLR 2025 —
   332	  verified BOTH at proceedings.iclr.cc (the review's exact link resolves to
   333	  this paper) and arXiv:2408.03314]. Accounting: FLOPs-matched, but on
   334	  MATH-class tasks with a trained verifier/reviser — the "matched" side does
   335	  not include verifier training compute. This is the strongest justification
   336	  for the frontier-builder's clause (iv) (adaptive test-time compute as a
   337	  MANDATORY comparator capability) and simultaneously a lifecycle-ledger
   338	  cautionary tale (KOT-LIFE/1 must log the verifier's training cost on OUR
   339	  side too) [STIPULATED].
   340	- **GPT-BERT beat the BabyLM baselines at matched data budget** (10M/100M
   340	- **GPT-BERT beat the BabyLM baselines at matched data budget** (10M/100M
   341	  words, same eval pipeline): BLiMP 81.2 vs 69.8 baseline at 10M [LIT-BACKED:
   342	  arXiv:2412.05149, CoNLL 2024]. Accounting: matched words, NOT matched
   343	  training FLOPs — the findings paper itself reports a strong FLOPs–performance
   344	  relationship across submissions, so part of the win is compute [LIT-BACKED:
   345	  same]. Lesson for P3-D-BASE: data-matched ≠ compute-matched; our
   346	  tuning-symmetry rule (total tuning compute) is the right axis.
   347	- **CLUTRR's GNN-over-symbolic-graphs beat BERT-over-text** on systematic
   348	  generalization and robustness [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:1908.06177, EMNLP 2019].
   349	  Accounting: the GNN receives ORACLE symbolic input — this is precisely an
   350	  `oracle-diagnostic` win in our terminology, not an NL-input win; it is the
   351	  2019 version of our own l3a/a5 lesson (exact machinery inside the wall,
   352	  boundary unpaid). It motivates the H-GNN family AND its ASM-0814 gating in
   353	  one datum [STIPULATED].
   354	- **Nothing in the composite-index literature shows a small model "beating"
   355	  matched baselines via indexing choices** — the reverse: metabench shows most
   356	  legacy-suite variance is one factor (§2), and GSM1k/rephrase results show
   357	  apparent wins evaporating on sealed replicas (§4). The methodological moral
   358	  for W1: index construction and sealing are where ILLUSORY wins are
   359	  manufactured or destroyed, which is why P3-D-INDEX/P3-D-SEAL gate every
   360	  W1-relevant experiment [STIPULATED].
   361	
   362	## 8. Open questions Phase-1 must resolve (this review's residue)
   363	
   364	1. **Ceiling pinning** (P3-D-INDEX): only BLiMP has a verified human ceiling;
   365	   pick and freeze ceilings for every other component (named reference model
   366	   own-harness scores), and decide the clamping rule when a system exceeds the
   367	   pinned ceiling.
   368	2. **Domain weights and the scalar** (P3-D-INDEX): equal-weight across D1–D7
   369	   vs coverage-proportional; metabench's one-factor result argues the scalar
   370	   should be de-emphasized in favor of the vector + per-domain LCBs.
   371	3. **Prompting-strategy pin** (P3-D-INDEX): BBH (and D4 suites) move
   372	   enormously with CoT; per-rung strategy must be frozen or the index is
   373	   gameable via strategy shopping — a threat-model row (P3-D-THREAT co-input).
   374	4. **Tiny-scale D4 floors are unmeasured in the literature** (P3-D-POWER /
   375	   P3-E-CAL): no published CLUTRR/ProofWriter zero-shot numbers at 135M–1.7B
   376	   were found; the calibration run must measure them before margins δ_k are
   377	   set. Fine-tuned-vs-prompted protocol for D4 must be decided (§6).
   378	5. **Consistency-band constant** (P3-D-SEAL): GSM1k's ≤8% honest-model gap is
   379	   the only published anchor; our band needs own-variance estimates from
   380	   P3-E-CAL seeds.
   381	6. **Proxy-rung claims per metric family** (P3-D-INDEX + G5): formalize
   382	   "continuous metrics extrapolate, MC accuracy does not" into the analysis
   383	   plan's cross-rung inference rules.
   384	7. **Sealed producer independence** (P3-D-SEAL): generator-based sealing is
   385	   cheap but WE author the generator — decide what "independent party" means
   386	   on this box (cross-vendor generation? maintainer-held seeds?), the one
   387	   governance question the literature cannot answer for us.
   388	8. **CLUTRR licence** (P3-D-INDEX): CC-BY-NC-4.0 — eval-internal use is fine;
   389	   no redistribution of derived artifacts; confirm the sealed refresh
   390	   (our own generator re-implementation vs their generator) doesn't create a
   391	   licensing dependency.
   392	
   393	## 9. Phase-1 hand-off — the design beads this review recommends the coordinator create
   394	
   395	- **P3-D-INDEX** — freeze KOT-AI-INDEX/2's concrete pins (suite membership,
   396	  domain assignment, normalization constants, ceilings, harness+prompt
   397	  strategy, analysis plan). *De-risked here:* the normalization mathematics

   404	- **P3-D-SEAL** — the sealed-eval protocol. *De-risked here:* three verified

   412	- **P3-D-POWER** — the coverage × max-gain power analysis. *De-risked here:*

   419	- **P3-D-BASE** — the baseline-family spec. *De-risked here:* adaptive

item.text:
## Verdict

Do **not** commit this as the final P3-LR-EVAL deliverable yet. It is a strong, decision-oriented draft with mostly real sources and good skepticism about contamination and unmatched-compute results, but several central design conclusions outrun the evidence. The sealed-evaluation gate and index/power mathematics need correction first.

## Three most important concerns, ranked

1. **The sealed evaluation does not yet validate a W1 win.**

   The proposed gate only requires “directional consistency” with the frozen suite, not that the system beat the resource-matched baseline on sealed data by a registered margin. A frozen-suite win could therefore survive weak or null sealed performance.

   The claimed “≤8% honest-model” consistency anchor is also wrong. GSM1k observed drops of up to 8% among the particular models tested; it did not define an acceptable gap for uncontaminated or “honest” models. Some families showed systematic overfitting, while frontier models showed smaller gaps. That result cannot become a universal tolerance band. [GSM1k paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.00332)

   P3-D-SEAL still needs:

   - sealed-side baseline comparisons and a registered effect/margin;
   - custody, access, one-shot-use and post-evaluation disclosure rules;
   - independent difficulty calibration by domain;
   - generator secrecy or genuinely held-out generator families—not merely fresh seeds from a known generator;
   - rules preventing feedback from one sealed release being used before the next claimed evaluation.

2. **The index and P3-D-POWER mathematics are not internally complete.**

   Chance normalization is reasonable and accurately traced to OLLv2, but reference/human “ceilings” are not actual ceilings. They are noisy scores under particular prompts and harnesses. They can produce unstable denominators, scores above 1, saturation if clamped, and rank changes when the reference is updated. BLiMP’s 88.6 individual-human and 96.4 aggregate agreement are different estimands, not interchangeable ceiling candidates. [BLiMP paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.00582)

   More specific problems:

   - Held-out LM loss, calibration, abstention/selective prediction, and robustness do not have the stated chance-to-ceiling accuracy normalization. Yet LM loss is an R-0 index component.
   - Hierarchical bootstrapping estimates uncertainty; it does **not** “handle different variances” in the aggregate. The weighting estimand remains unresolved.
   - Resampling benchmark families only makes sense if they are treated as samples from a larger benchmark universe. For a fixed registered suite, use fixed domain/benchmark weights and bootstrap items, prompts/seeds, and paired outputs within benchmarks.
   - The G1 formula `κ(1−a_cov)` is on a raw-accuracy scale. Under the proposed normalized score, the maximum covered-item contribution generally needs the relevant normalization denominator and domain/benchmark weights. It also needs uncertainty in κ and covered-subset accuracy.
   - Below-floor components should not simply be removed after looking at results; the floor/exclusion criterion must be registered from calibration data.

3. **The small-model floor claims and handoff claims are overstated.**

   The document says D1–D7 placements are floor-validated, but it does not validate D2/D3/D7 comprehensively, and several D4 floors remain explicitly open. MATH, GPQA and modern prompted CLUTRR/ProofWriter floors are unverified.

   The BBH inference is particularly unsafe: SmolLM2’s lighteval aggregate is combined with OLLv2’s adapted per-subtask choice counts to estimate a 25–30% floor. Those are not established as the same task representation, metric or aggregation. The original BBH paper has 23 tasks, while the OLLv2 implementation/documentation uses its own task collection and normalization. The card’s 28.2/32.2 numbers alone do not prove near-floor normalized performance.

   Likewise, “OLLv2 floors out at 100M–2B” conflicts with the review’s own 1.7B MMLU-Pro result of 19.3% against 10% chance. That may still be too weak for the chosen rung, but it is not demonstrated to be floor performance.

## Source and factual audit

I spot-checked HELM, OLLv2 normalization/components, metabench, GSM1k, rephrased contamination, Oren et al., Snell et al., BLiMP, FOLIO, BabyLM and the SmolLM2 card.

No clearly hallucinated paper was found. Most central numerical claims are real:

- HELM’s 42 scenarios, 16 core scenarios, seven metrics and 17.9%→96.0% coverage are supported. However, the paper says the seven metrics were applied to core scenarios **when possible—87.5% of the time**, not unconditionally “under 7 metrics per core scenario.” [HELM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.09110)
- OLLv2’s chance normalization, zero-clamping and per-subtask normalization are accurately described. [OLLv2 normalization](https://huggingface.co/docs/leaderboards/en/open_llm_leaderboard/normalization)
- The metabench 1.24%/0.58%/0.94 figures match the accepted ICLR version. Its one-factor result supports redundancy in those six benchmarks, but it does not establish that D4 is the only capability outside that factor or validate the proposed D1–D7 ontology. [Accepted metabench paper](https://openreview.net/forum?id=4T33izzFpK)
- The paraphrase-contamination and Oren exchangeability claims are substantially accurate. Oren detects canonical-order memorization; it is not a general detector of semantic or shuffled-item contamination, which makes the review’s negative-result warning important. [Oren et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.17623)
- Snell et al. is correctly identified as FLOPs-matched inference work, and the review commendably flags omitted verifier training cost. But the 14× result is conditional: it applies where the smaller model already has non-trivial success and depends strongly on prompt difficulty and verifier quality. It is not a blanket small-model win. [Snell et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.03314)
- FOLIO’s construction is correctly cited, but “GPT-4 struggles on a subset, therefore ≤1.7B should be at chance” is unsupported extrapolation, not floor evidence. [FOLIO](https://aclanthology.org/2024.emnlp-main.1229/)
- “Continuous metrics carry predictive signal upward” is too strong. The cited papers show that metric transformations can hide smooth scaling and that some observational relationships are predictable; they do not license cross-rung prediction for BLiMP/EWoK without empirical validation. [Schaeffer et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.15004), [Ruan et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.10938)

Ledger issues:

- All five JSONL `verified:false` records are visibly marked false. Good.
- Partial verification is represented inconsistently: MATH tiny-scale floors have no source record, while venue details are unverified inside otherwise `verified:true` records.
- The prose reports “24” verified sources, while the JSONL contains 27 `verified:true` entries. This may be 24 distinct works plus corroborating/card entries, but it should say so.
- “Every chance floor is verified” is false for the entire proposed index: generative zero is a convention, LM loss has no such chance floor, and several proposed domain components are absent from the floor table.

## Methodological assessment

The review is genuinely decision-oriented. Its strongest conclusions are:

- publish the domain vector with any scalar;
- normalize heterogeneous multiple-choice tasks within benchmark/subtask before aggregation;
- treat overlap screening as a gross-leakage screen, not certification;
- require sealed evaluation;
- pin prompting and harness choices;
- distinguish prompted from fine-tuned benchmark performance;
- reject unmatched-data or oracle-input wins as evidence of end-to-end superiority;
- include adaptive test-time compute among baseline candidates where applicable.

Within-domain macro-averaging is defensible if domain importance is explicitly fixed, but it is a normative weighting choice—not a statistical consequence of HELM or metabench. Equal domain weight, benchmark weight within domain, missing-domain handling and cross-rung comparability all remain unresolved.

## Phase-1 handoffs

- **P3-D-INDEX:** partly de-risked, but still missing metric-specific transforms, reference-score uncertainty, clamping policy, fixed weights, floor criteria, abstention scoring and full D1–D7 calibration.
- **P3-D-SEAL:** useful recipes, but not yet a production protocol or valid claim gate.
- **P3-D-POWER:** insufficiently de-risked. It needs corrected normalized-scale bounds, uncertainty in coverage, suite covariance, registered δ values and actual sample-size/power calculations.
- **P3-D-BASE:** appropriately warns about verifier training and data-vs-compute matching, but does not by itself specify credible rung baselines. It remains dependent on P3-LR-RAG, P3-LR-TINY and P3-D-FRONTIER.

The unmatched-compute discussion is one of the better parts: it does not simply accept the BabyLM, CLUTRR or Snell wins. The main overstatement is calling adaptive test-time compute “literature-mandatory” for every frontier; it should be mandatory to consider and include where task-appropriate and budget-feasible.

**Commit recommendation:** revise first. The sources are mostly sound, but the current text overclaims floor validation and turns exploratory precedents into insufficiently specified index, power and sealed-evaluation rules. After those targeted corrections, it should be suitable as P3-LR-EVAL.
